one_line_takeaway: MOLLM reframes GA-based LLM unlearning as a three-objective optimization solved with a bounded UCE loss and a dual-space common-descent direction, cutting LLaMA3-8B's harmful rate from 79.18% to 3.45% while preserving (even improving) fluency and eliminating gradient conflict.
key_finding: On LLaMA3-8B / PKU-SafeRLHF (Table I), MOLLM achieves the best trade-off: it lowers Harmful Rate from the original 79.18% to 3.45% (vs GA+Weighted-sum 5.46%, GA+OGD 3.68%, Relabeling 5.72%, Re-finetuning only 53.92%), reaches the lowest Toxicity 0.007 and Obscenity 0.002, and importantly the best Fluency 1.689 (lower than the original model's 1.754, i.e. utility preserved), whereas GA+OGD reaches a comparable HR of 3.68% but degrades Fluency to 4.490. MOLLM drives all three conflict probabilities PC_fgt = PC_KL = PC_rt = 0, versus GA+Weighted-sum (PC_fgt 19.87%, PC_KL 22.32%) and GA+OGD (PC_rt 63.46%), empirically confirming it eliminates gradient conflict. Ablations show removing UCE (Ablation 1) causes catastrophic forgetting (Fluency 4.266) and using only -g_fgt (Ablation 3) worsens Fluency to 5.08, validating that both the UCE loss and the common-descent direction are necessary.
core_contribution: The paper proposes MOLLM (Multi-Objective LLM Unlearning), a Gradient-Ascent-based machine-unlearning method for removing undesirable (e.g., harmful) behaviors from an already-trained LLM without full retraining. It makes three distinct own contributions: (1) an Unlearning Cross-Entropy (UCE) loss, L_UCE = -(1/K) Σ_i Σ_c y_{i,c} log(1-(1-ε)p_{i,c}), which replaces the unbounded inverse-CE loss used by standard Gradient Ascent; because UCE has a lower bound of 0 and is minimized by ordinary gradient descent, it prevents the gradient explosion that GA suffers, without the extra gradient-clipping hyperparameter tuning; (2) formulating LLM unlearning as a three-objective optimization problem min_θ {L_fgt, L_KL, L_rt} (forget loss, KL-divergence to the original model on retain data, and CE loss on retain data); and (3) DS-MGDA (Dual Space Multiple Gradient Descent Algorithm), which computes a single common-descent update direction by projecting each gradient onto the null space of the other two (g*_fgt = g_fgt - A^T(AA^T)^{-1}A g_fgt) and averaging the negated dual vectors, so the update simultaneously decreases all three objectives and avoids the gradient conflict that plagues the naive weighted-sum aggregation. NOTE: GA itself, the L_KL term, and L_rt are prior work the authors build on (cited to Yao et al. [6] and Chen & Yang [10]); OGD [27] and relabeling [28] are baselines, not this paper's methods.
threat_model: This is a defense/safety paper. The concern is that a deployed LLM has memorized undesirable data — harmful/toxic responses, and (as motivation) copyright-infringing text, hallucination-inducing content, and private user data — whose influence must be removed after training. Full retraining is assumed too costly, so the "adversary" is effectively the residual undesirable behavior itself plus the practical obstacle that naive Gradient Ascent unlearning either explodes gradients or catastrophically destroys retained utility. MOLLM is the mitigation: selectively remove the forget set's influence (measured by reduced harmful rate/toxicity) while keeping utility on the retain set intact. It does not model an active attacker or membership-inference/extraction adversary; unlearning verification is behavioral (harmful rate, toxicity) rather than a formal privacy guarantee.
limitations: Scope is narrow: a single model (LLaMA3-8B) and a single dataset (PKU-SafeRLHF), so generalization across model sizes, architectures, and unlearning domains (copyright, privacy, hallucination — all cited as motivation but never tested) is unverified. Evaluation of unlearning success is purely behavioral (harmful rate, Detoxify toxicity/obscenity, fluency) with no membership-inference / extraction / privacy-guarantee test, so it does not show the forgotten data is truly unrecoverable — only that harmful outputs drop. No formal convergence or optimality proof is provided for DS-MGDA beyond citing the general multiple-gradient-descent Pareto-stationarity theorem; DS-MGDA also requires the gradient matrix A to be full-rank, which is asserted but not analyzed. The UCE loss introduces a scalar ε whose sensitivity is not studied. Compute/runtime cost of the extra projection and multi-gradient computation is not reported. Baselines are limited to GA-family and simple relabeling/re-finetuning; stronger non-GA unlearning methods are discussed in related work but not compared empirically. Differences among the top methods on HR are small (3.45% vs 3.68%), so the headline gain is mainly in jointly holding fluency and conflict low rather than a large HR margin.

datasets_used: PKU-SafeRLHF (safety alignment preference dataset for LLaMA-family models, built on BeaverTails)
quantitative_results: Harmful Rate reduced from Original 79.18% to MOLLM 3.45% (GA+Weighted-sum 5.46%, GA+OGD 3.68%, Relabeling 5.72%, Re-finetuning 53.92%)
MOLLM Fluency 1.689, better (lower) than Original 1.754; GA+OGD degrades Fluency to 4.490
MOLLM conflict probabilities PC_fgt=PC_KL=PC_rt=0%, vs GA+Weighted-sum PC_fgt=19.87%/PC_KL=22.32% and GA+OGD PC_rt=63.46%
Ablation without UCE (CE loss): Fluency 4.266 (catastrophic forgetting); Ablation using only -g_fgt: Fluency 5.08
Model tested: LLaMA3-8B; learning rate from {1e-5, 5e-6, 1e-6}, decay 0.999/round
baselines_compared: Re-finetuning on retain set from scratch
